message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The Covid-19 pandemic has been marked by considerable heterogeneity in outbreaks across the United States. Local factors that may be associated with variation in SARS-CoV-2 transmission have not been well studied.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To examine the association of county-level factors with variation in the SARS-CoV-2 reproduction number over time.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Observational study</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>211 counties in 46 states and the District of Columbia between February 25, 2020 and April 23, 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Residents within the counties (55% of the US population)</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Social distancing as measured by percent change in visits to non-essential businesses, population density, lagged daily wet bulb temperatures.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcomes and Measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The instantaneous reproduction number (R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    ) which is the estimated number of cases generated by one case at a given time during the pandemic.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Median case incidence was 1185 cases and fatality rate was 43.7 deaths per 100,000 people for the top decile of 21 counties, nearly ten times the incidence and fatality rate in the lowest density quartile. Average R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    in the first two weeks was 5.7 (SD 2.5) in the top decile, compared to 3.1 (SD 1.2) in the lowest quartile. In multivariable analysis, a 50% decrease in visits to non-essential businesses was associated with a 57% decrease in R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    (95% confidence interval, 56% to 58%). Cumulative temperature effects over 4 to 10 days prior to case incidence were nonlinear; relative R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    decreased as temperatures warmed above 32°F to 53°F, which was the point of minimum R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    , then increased between 53°F and 66°F, at which point R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    began to decrease. At 55°F, and with a 70% reduction in visits to non-essential business, 96% of counties were estimated to fall below a threshold R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    of 1.0, including 86% of counties among the top density decile and 98% of counties in the lowest density quartile.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Social distancing, lower population density, and temperate weather change were associated with a decreased SARS-Co-V-2 R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    in counties across the United States. These relationships can inform selective public policy planning in communities during the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key Points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      How is the instantaneous reproduction number (R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                      ) of SARS-CoV-2 influenced by local area effects of social distancing, wet bulb temperature, and population density in counties across the United States?
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      Social distancing, temperate weather, and lower population density were associated with a decrease in R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                      . Of these county-specific factors, social distancing appeared to be the most significant in reducing SARS-CoV-2 transmission.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                      varies significantly across counties. The relationship between R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                      and county-specific factors can inform policies to reduce SARS-CoV-2 transmission in selective and heterogeneous communities.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: The Association of Social Distancing, Population Density, and Temperature with the SARS-CoV-2 Instantaneous Reproduction Number in Counties Across the United States

reference.unstructured: Coronavirus (Covid-19) Data in the United States. The New York Times. https://github.com/nytimes/covid-19-data. Published 2020. Accessed April 28, 2020.
Unacast. Unacast Social Distancing Dataset. In: Unacast, ed. 17 April 2020 ed2020.
